Let's talk about the lean and mean fast cutting way to get through the gatekeeper. Okay. Very simple. I perfected this back in the late 80s. It still works like a chomps and use it other companies in the last five years. Okay. Basically, you want the gatekeeper to think that you're a personal friend of the person you're calling. That's it. All right, guys. And I don't mean that unethically. You're not going to lie. You're not what you want. And everything you say, everything you do, meaning your demeanor, your voice, your tonalities, the way you approach the call, your confidence, how you position yourself as an expert, all designed that the gatekeeper would think that maybe he should, he or she should put you through because you're a friend or a contemporary of the person. That's the bottom line. So if I'm calling for a CEO of XYZ companies, his name is John Smith, I will not call him and say, hi, I'm looking for Mr. John Smith, please. The second you do that, there's, there's, iniquivocally, no, no, um, you know, dispute in the mind of the gatekeeper. This is a sales call. You're looking to sell the guy some and their job is, is they are there to keep you out. Now, some people will tell you that are just full of sales. Well, no, they're there to screen out which sales office are right. That's a load of guys. I've been on the other side. I'm the CEO. I tell my people, don't put any sales. I don't want to speak to them. Again, I want to speak to them. If they get through, they got to do it ingeniously. You're not going to get through my assistant. Okay. I don't want to hear about it. I'll go to someone else first, go to a lower level first and work their way up. So the point is, if you want to get through to, if you really want to get through to someone, you got to position yourself as not a salesperson. So watch, I call up Mr. John Smith and say, Hey, is John there? That's it. Hey, is John there? Hey, John, please. Hey, let me see the John, please. In other words, everything, what did I do there? No magic words. It's just like, Hey, John, how would you call and speak to your friend? If you, if your friend worked at a company, you were calling your friend, and what would you say to his secretary? If you never spoke to him, say, Hey, let me see the John, please. Hey, is John there? That you would say, Hello, I am looking for Mr. John Smith, please. It's Jordan Belville, XYZ. You would never say that. So you want to position yourself as a friend or personal acquaintance of the CEO. All right. Now, the key here is in the silence. In other words, what happens is, is one of those questions where, you know, whoever speaks first loses. So you call up a gatekeeper, they say, Hi, Mr. Smith's office. Say, Hey, is John there? They'll say who's, and they say, well, who's calling? Oh, it's Jordan. That's it. Jordan. Nothing else. They'll be like, Oh, what the fuck? Jordan. Oh, Jordan. Belville. He'll know. He'll know. He'll know what his reference to. That's it. Now you're saying, aren't they pissed at you? If you, if they said you, they knew what it was about. No, they just won't. I'm doing this a million times. And just so you know, the amount of extra people you get through, okay, by doing that. Now, again, I don't want you to lie. I want you to say, you know, personal business, okay, you know, try to, I hate this guy. I gotta be, I hate saying anything here that can be being misconstrued as being unethical. But the bottom line is you've got to be a little bit cagey with these people because they're being cagey with you. You get it. They're designed, they're thwart you. They're not your best friend. Okay. Now in some situations, when you, as a last resort, you'll go that route as well, especially, you know, high ticket business. But if someone's teaching you, that's what they do. They're just playing wrong. It doesn't work. It'll be nice in a bubble world, but in the real world of sales, that's not the way it goes because any well-trained gatekeeper is designed to keep you out, not if you befriend them that they'll let you through.
